semantics: The pinned JSC__Wasm__StreamingCompiler__addBytes export retains its exact C calling convention, opaque compiler token, byte pointer, byte length, and void result.
Opaque handles are monotonically issued nonzero registry tokens and are never dereferenced or reused, so null, forged, and stale caller values fail closed.
Every record retains both its creating Context identity and its VM identity; sibling contexts in one group may finalize it while an independent VM is rejected.
A non-empty span requires a non-null pointer and is copied before the call returns; the caller retains no backing-storage lifetime obligation.
Zero-length input is an exact no-op and accepts a null pointer.
Ordered calls append ordered bytes; concurrent calls are serialized into complete spans with unspecified inter-span order and no torn data.
Length overflow and allocation failure are sticky and failure-atomic: existing bytes remain unchanged and later appends are inert.
The upstream void addBytes ABI makes invalid, failed, and finalized calls inert; status is observable only through the zig-js lifecycle extensions.
Finalization is idempotent, freezes the byte vector exactly once, reports the required size, and copies only into sufficient caller-owned storage.
Release validates VM ownership, removes the registry record exactly once, and makes every later feed, finalize, or release fail closed.
Context teardown retires every unreleased compiler created by that Context before its Wasm store and allocator disappear.

The JavaScript compileStreaming and instantiateStreaming path creates this lifecycle before the first body pull, feeds each actual Response chunk in order, finalizes at EOF, releases it, and then dispatches the ordinary compiler jobs.
The registry and every state transition are protected by one process-wide lock and use a thread-safe allocator, making cross-VM calls and concurrent feed operations TSan-clean.

External Home and Bun fixtures link the exact pinned symbol and exercise ordered, zero-length, invalid-pointer, overflow, finalized, stale, forged, sibling, foreign, concurrent, and teardown boundaries.
Focused Debug, ReleaseSafe, and TSan gates run both external ownership profiles and the JavaScript streaming compilation path.
